verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (heat shock protein family D member 1, aka HSP60) is a mitochondrial chaperone resident in the matrix and inner membrane. However, it has documented surface display under stress and immune conditions: (1) cell-stress-induced externalization — hypoxia, oxidative stress, and ER stress trigger translocation of HSP60 to the outer leaflet; (2) immunogenic cell death — apoptototic and necroptotic cells externalize HSP60 on the PM as a danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP); (3) cancer ecto-display — tumor cells display HSP60 on the outer leaflet, where it serves as a tumor-associated antigen and immune target. While canonically intracellular/mitochondrial, the documented stress-state and cell-death-induced ecto-pool qualifies as contextual surface accessibility.
